title_r: A direct replication of ego depletion
abstract_r: This study attempts a direct replication of Baumeister et al. (1998). Participants completed two tasks in sequence. Contrary to the original findings, we found no evidence of ego depletion (d = 0.04). The original study reported a significant effect of prior self-control on subsequent task performance.
is_replication: direct replication of Baumeister et al. (1998)

abstract_r: We conducted a direct replication of Strack et al. (1988) pen-in-mouth paradigm across 17 labs. The replication found a significant but smaller effect than the original study reported.
source: Journal of Personality and Social Psychology
is_reproduction: direct replication of Strack et al. (1988)

abstract_r: This paper replicates Mischel et al. (1972) delay of gratification experiment with a larger and more diverse sample. Results suggest the predictive validity of delay of gratification is substantially smaller than originally reported.

title_r: Estimating the reproducibility of psychological science
abstract_r: We attempted to replicate 100 experimental and correlational studies published in three psychology journals. Replication effects were half the magnitude of original effects on average.

title_r: Replication of the cognitive reflection test
abstract_r: A pre-registered replication of Frederick (2005) cognitive reflection test across 8 countries. The three-item CRT was successfully replicated with similar effect sizes.
We conducted a direct replication of the original Stroop color-word interference experiment. The effect was robustly replicated across three independent samples (d = 1.10).

title_r: A replication attempt of mortality salience effects
abstract_r: This registered replication report attempts to replicate Rosenblatt et al. (1989) study on mortality salience and judgments of moral transgressions across 8 labs.
is_reproduction: attempts to replicate Rosenblatt et al. (1989)

abstract_r: Ranehill et al. (2015) attempted a replication of Carney et al. (2010) power pose study. The replication found effects on self-reported feelings but not on hormone levels.
We attempted to replicate three of Bem's (2011) precognition experiments. No evidence was found for precognition in any of the three experiments (all p > .30).
is_reproduction: replicate three of Bem's (2011) precognition experiments

title_r: A replication of the implicit association test
abstract_r: We report a replication of Greenwald et al. (1998) Implicit Association Test with a sample of N=500. The IAT demonstrated strong psychometric properties consistent with original reports.
